speaker: interviewee
text: On the top, on the-- yes? It is the Kierszniewski's home. The Kierszniewskis were-- Roman Kierszniewski, my really, truly treasured them, very much loved them. Roman Kierszniewski, is no more alive. He was about 80 when he died. And this is his widow, Irena, who was a beautiful girl. You made a photograph before of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle. I embrace her of my-- and this is her daughter-in-law on the left-hand side. I embrace her. This is on my visit to Poland in May, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: 50 Years later.

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 years, yeah, later. And we are marvelous friends. This is one. And the bottom one-- And-- and this picture is, again, in their home. Of course, on the same morning or afternoon. And I'm standing next to Irena. And Vivian is next to her and Peter, because they went with me to Poland. And they-- they came, and we visited all the memorials. And on the-- on my left-hand side is the-- my-- Irena's granddaughter-- one of her granddaughters. She has two. And down here is her daughter-in-law, and it is in their home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes? On the left-hand side is the picture of Roman and Irena and Andrew Kierszniewski. This is how I knew Andrew, and he was four years old. Irena was lovely, beautiful. And Roman-- this is, I think, a picture taken a little earlier, maybe just at-- at the beginning of the war or just before the-- immediately before the war. But they look almost like I-- when I have met them. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Irena and Andrew, who is 54 years old. And Irena is, I think, 82. And he has got an allotment near Warsaw. And he took me to this allotment. It is in front of us, and it is near the forest and they grow there, you know, vegetables and flowers. He spends a lot of time there. This is taken on the same day, on this allotment, which is showing part of it, where Andrew grew-- Andrew grows flowers and vegetables. And I'm embracing my dear friend Irena. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is only one-- the only piece left. Of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the wall, which encircled the ghetto, the small ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: The small ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The small-- where I was, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I and my daughter, Vivian and I. And that was, sort of, I don't know, blessed or something by the president of Israel, by Chaim Herzog.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken when?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to visit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the medal of the ghetto. To you. You see? [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] OK, what's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the memorial, which is erected after the war by, the Polish-- I don't know, Polish people. Probably Polish people, because there are very hardly any Jews there left. And this is this Umschlagplatz. This is the memorial where, in the-- in front of this Umschlagplatz. No, they had the Umschlagplatz, which used to be-- you can't see this now. It's built up. It was behind. And in front, was the-- was the railway station. The railway was-- which took people directly to Treblinka. This is exactly that. And it is written there that 300,000 people perished there in Treblinka. Only Treblinka, yes, 300,000. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, And there's some of the names of the people taken at random who perished there in Treblinka-- different name. I looked at this, but you see, it's written-- 300,000 people. 300,000 people were taken from this Umschlagplatz, from the Warsaw ghetto, from both ghettos. You see? Because from the small ghetto, they took to the big ghetto, to Treblinka-- directly to Treblinka. It-- it is-- it is absolutely hard to believe, when I recollect, that-- that it has happened. Such a-- such a catastrophe. Such a catastrophe. Such an unbelievable event-- which I think has, forever and ever, changed the face of-- of the world. It did. It did. It has taught people how you can-- how you can dehumanize the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the people you don't want to be or you don't like them. Like in-- in Uganda or like in-- what is the name now? Rwanda.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rwanda, Rwanda, sorry. Rwanda or like in [INAUDIBLE], Yugoslavia. You showed what you can do. Hitler showed the world what you can do to another human being, and I think the world will never, never be the same, unfortunately. It is-- it is, Rena, it is on the way. This was guiding us. We were told when we went with Vivian and Peter to Poland. And Peter was insistent that we should go and try to find the cemetery where I buried my-- my brother, Rudolph. And slowly and slowly, gradually, we found out from people that there exist-- existed a-- a small Jewish cemetery. And it is written, Jewish cemetery-- in Anielinie. In Anielinie. Anielinie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Anielinie?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little-- a name of the little-- of the-- of the locality, of the-- of the village there-- in Anielinie near Otvotsk. And we were guided. We were traveling for miles and miles by this taxi, and suddenly, we have seen this. And you see with the Jewish that David's Star.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we followed this. And as we followed it, I remembered the lane-- how I walked under the-- after the coffin of my brother, with this only one, with the-- with the carpenter. The horse, the cart, the carpenter, and I. And I remember it was a small, sunny lane. And on the left-hand side was the cemetery and we found it after nearly an hour. And that was the cemetery on that second picture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And here is-- here is, you know, part of the-- it is now a small-- I immediately recognized it. And it is a-- it is an enclosure. And on the other pictures I have, there are-- there are, you know, graves, plates of graves, which are-- which are, some of them, very neglected. And some of them are still standing, but most of them are neglected. It's a-- a secluded place in a-- in a no man's land somewhere. With all this-- a lot of sunshine, as you can see. The same-- the same-- it was always this bright sunshine and a lot of greenery. And people stopped being buried there in 1940, because in 1940, Hitler started sending people to extermination camp. Since 1940, this cemetery doesn't anymore exist, but his bones are somewhere there. And Peter-- Dr. Peter Lawrence, my son-in-law-- was-- you see, he wears this-- this little hat, Kippah and he says-- and he says Kaddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: For your--

speaker: interviewee
text: For my-- for my brother. Which is-- Kaddish is the prayer for the-- for the deaths, the Jewish prayer for the deaths, yes, for my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: After 50 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: After 50 years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are happier times, thank God and touch wood. Touch wood till 120. This is my lovely, very devoted daughter, Vivian, and her husband, Dr. Peter Lawrence. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken on, I think, a-- a very special occasion of some type. Oh, it must have been maybe four or five years ago, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken on the occasion of my youngest grandson, Simon. My lovely grandson, Simon, who's-- it was his bar mitzvah. When was his-- he's on the left-hand side. And in the middle is Jessica, His sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is a student at Nottingham University. And the first one is Mattiew, Matthiew Michael. His second name is after my beloved little brother, Michael. Matthiew Michael. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Matthiew Michael, and he-- he has just, as told you, he finished his university at Sheffield and tries and hopes he can do his PhD at Oxford. Lovely three of them. And the little one was relieved, because he was-- or was it before? He was going to-- to say the portion in the synagogue and he was very nervous. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Wait for you now.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] not working.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Hanna, would you like to present your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with greatest, greatest pleasure. This is Alex-- Alex Lifschutz, my lovely, beloved son, who, even when he was a little boy, a little baby, he was always wonderful, always sweet and kind. And he he was-- he was excellent at school, always, one after another-- symposium and the university and one degree and second degree. And he's an architect, who has got an established firm in London, and he's quite successful, thank God. Thank God for that. And his latest-- one of the latest achievements is this very famous restaurant in Oxo building, and the whole Oxo building-- the whole of Oxo building. And of course, building some bridges and changing the whole out-- the whole traffic outside Waterloo Bridge. So as I said before, not only, I would say predominately, I would say, not grateful but-- not proud, but very grateful for his consistent help, for making for me this lovely home, for taking care, for taking away and dealing with the troublesome problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: And producing this-- marrying lovely girl-- this lovely girl, Monique, who they are suited ideally together. And we have a fantastic relationship. And they're producing these two fantastic grandchildren. What-- what can I ask more? And you heard, probably, your mother's life story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, and as you can tell from the last speech, she's a very accomplished liar. But I'm delighted that she had the opportunity to speak tonight and that she has a-- a tape that tells her very remarkable story, which is an instruction and an example to all of us. And that's the most important thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- it is-- it is-- it is for you to keep it, for you to-- to remember it, and you will remember it.

speaker: interviewee
text: For our children.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the future generation, we hope. And we hope that the-- that Israel can be in peace. And be-- be in peace, the main-- the main thing. That's what we all need. We need peace. We need shalom. And I-- on this occasion, I want to thank very much, Rena, who has been a charming interviewer, and James for his patience and excellence in hopefully, producing a wonderful tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thank you. We thank you very, very much. And I wish you, again, all the best,

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till 120 and shalom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you. And shalom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Shalom.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] .
